title: Differentiation, Integration, Probability, Statistics, and Linear Algebra Refresher

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Differentiate term by term using \(\frac{d}{dx}x^n=nx^{n-1}\).
hints: Apply the power rule to each term; constants differentiate to zero.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Combine the results.
hints: The power rule also applies to negative and fractional exponents.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Differentiate to get \(-10x^{-3}+2x^{-2}\).
hints: Rewrite quotients as negative powers.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Apply the power rule.
hints: Convert the radical to a fractional exponent.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Treat \(\pi\) and \(e^2\) as constants.
Differentiate each term.
hints: Both \(\pi\) and \(e^2\) are constants.

questions.question: Find the tangent line at \(x=2\).
solutionSteps: Differentiate: \(y'=3x^2-2\).
The slope at \(x=2\) is \(10\).
The point is \((2,5)\).
Use point-slope form: \(y-5=10(x-2)\).
hints: Find both the point on the curve and the derivative at that point.

questions.question: Find all points where the graph has a horizontal tangent.
solutionSteps: Set \(f'(x)=3x^2-6x-9=3(x-3)(x+1)\) equal to zero.
Evaluate the function to obtain \(f(-1)=9\) and \(f(3)=-23\).
hints: Horizontal tangents occur where the derivative is zero.

questions.question: Determine where the function is increasing and decreasing.
solutionSteps: Compute \(f'(x)=2x-6\).
hints: Use the sign of the first derivative.

questions.question: Find the critical points.
solutionSteps: Set the derivative equal to zero.
hints: Factor the derivative completely.

questions.question: Use the derivative definition to find \(f'(x)\).
solutionSteps: Start with \(f'(x)=\lim_{h\to0}\frac{f(x+h)-f(x)}{h}\).
The numerator simplifies to \(2xh+h^2+3h\).
Divide by \(h\) and take the limit to get \(2x+3\).
hints: Expand \(f(x+h)\), cancel, and factor out \(h\).

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Use \((uv)'=u'v+uv'\).
Substitute \(u=x^2+1\) and \(v=3x^4-2x\).
Simplify the result.
hints: Use the product rule before simplifying.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Use the product rule on \(x^3(x+2)^{1/2}\).
Obtain \(3x^2\sqrt{x+2}+\frac{x^3}{2\sqrt{x+2}}\).
Combine into a single fraction.
hints: Use both the product rule and chain rule.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Apply \(\left(\frac{u}{v}\right)'=\frac{u'v-uv'}{v^2}\).
hints: Apply the quotient rule carefully.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Apply the quotient rule.
Use \(\frac{d}{dx}\sqrt{x}=\frac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}\).
Combine and simplify the numerator.
hints: A common denominator in the numerator helps simplify.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Differentiate the outer sixth power.
Multiply by the derivative of \(5x^2-3x+1\).
hints: Differentiate the outer function, then the inner function.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Apply the chain rule and simplify.
hints: Treat the square root as a one-half power.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Apply the chain rule to the fourth power.
The derivative of \(\frac{x+1}{x-1}\) is \(-\frac{2}{(x-1)^2}\).
Multiply and simplify.
hints: Differentiate the inner quotient first.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Use the product rule.
Differentiate \((x^3+1)^{-2}\) with the chain rule.
Factor and combine over a common denominator.
hints: Factor \(2x(x^3+1)^{-3}\) from the result.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Apply the chain rule.
\(\frac{3}{2}(x^2+4x)^{1/2}(2x+4)\) simplifies to the answer.
hints: Factor \(2\) from the derivative of the inner function.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Apply the quotient rule.
The numerator derivative is \(10(2x-1)^4\).
Factor \((2x-1)^4\) and simplify.
hints: Factor before fully expanding.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Use \(\frac{d}{dx}e^{u}=e^u u'\).
hints: Multiply by the derivative of the exponent.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Apply the chain rule to the exponent \(x^2-4x\).
hints: The derivative of the exponent is \(2x-4\).

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Use the product rule.
Factor out \(e^{-x}\).
hints: Use the product rule and then factor.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Use the standard derivative of the natural logarithm.
hints: Recall \((\ln x)'=1/x\).

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Use \((\ln u)'=u'/u\).
Here \(u'=10x\).
hints: Differentiate the inside and divide by the inside.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Use the product rule: \(1\cdot\ln x+x\cdot\frac1x\).
Simplify.
hints: Apply the product rule.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Rewrite as \(\ln(x+1)-\ln(x-1)\).
Differentiate and combine the fractions.
hints: Use logarithm quotient properties first.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Use \(\frac{d}{dx}a^x=a^x\ln a\).
hints: For a non-\(e\) base, include \(\ln a\).

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Let \(y=x^x\) and take logarithms: \(\ln y=x\ln x\).
Differentiate implicitly: \(y'/y=\ln x+1\).
Multiply by \(y=x^x\).
hints: Take natural logarithms before differentiating.

questions.question: Use logarithmic differentiation.
solutionSteps: Take logarithms: \(\ln f=3\ln x+\frac12\ln(x+1)-2\ln(x^2+4)\).
Differentiate both sides.
Multiply by \(f(x)\).
hints: Turn products, quotients, and powers into sums using logarithms.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Use \((\sin x)'=\cos x\) and \((\cos x)'=-\sin x\).
hints: Watch the two negative signs in the cosine term.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Differentiate \(\tan u\) as \(\sec^2u\cdot u'\).
hints: Use the chain rule.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Differentiate the outer sine, then multiply by \(2x\).
hints: Apply the chain rule.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Use the product rule.
\((\cos x)'=-\sin x\).
hints: Differentiate both factors using the product rule.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Treat the function as \([\sec(3x-1)]^2\).
Apply the chain rule twice and simplify.
hints: There is an outer square and an inner linear function.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Rewrite as \((\sin x)^3\) and apply the chain rule.
hints: Differentiate the outer cube first.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Apply the quotient rule.
The numerator simplifies to \(-\sin x(1+\sin x)-\cos^2x=-(1+\sin x)\).
Cancel a common factor.
hints: Use \(\sin^2x+\cos^2x=1\) when simplifying.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Use \((\arctan u)'=\frac{u'}{1+u^2}\).
hints: Here \(u=4x\).

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Use \((\arcsin u)'=\frac{u'}{\sqrt{1-u^2}}\).
Substitute \(u=x^2\).
hints: Square the entire inner function in the denominator.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Use the product rule.
hints: Apply the product rule and remember the negative derivative of arccosine.

questions.question: Find \(\frac{dy}{dx}\).
solutionSteps: Differentiate implicitly: \(2x+2yy'=0\).
Solve for \(y'\).
hints: Differentiate \(y^2\) using the chain rule.

questions.question: Find the slope at \((1,2)\).
solutionSteps: Differentiate: \(2x+y+xy'+2yy'=0\).
Substitute \((1,2)\).
hints: Remember that \((xy)'=x y'+y\).

questions.question: Find \(\frac{dy}{dx}\).
solutionSteps: Differentiate: \(3x^2+3y^2y'=6y+6xy'\).
Collect the \(y'\) terms and solve.
hints: Move all terms containing \(y'\) to one side.

questions.question: Find \(\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}\).
solutionSteps: First, \(y'=-x/y\).
Differentiate \(-x/y\) using the quotient rule.
Use \(x^2+y^2=16\) to simplify.
hints: Substitute the original equation near the end.

questions.question: A circle's radius increases at \(3\text{ cm/s}\). How fast is its area increasing when \(r=8\text{ cm}\)?
solutionSteps: Differentiate \(A=\pi r^2\): \(dA/dt=2\pi r\,dr/dt\).
Substitute \(r=8\) and \(dr/dt=3\).
hints: Differentiate with respect to time.

questions.question: A 10-meter ladder slides down a wall. The bottom moves away at \(2\text{ m/s}\). How fast is the top moving when the bottom is 6 meters from the wall?
solutionSteps: Differentiate: \(2x\,dx/dt+2y\,dy/dt=0\).
Substitute the known values and solve.
hints: Use the Pythagorean relationship and note that downward velocity is negative.

questions.question: Find the local extrema.
solutionSteps: Use \(f''(x)=6x-12\) to classify the critical points.
Evaluate \(f(1)\) and \(f(3)\).
hints: Use the second derivative test.

questions.question: Find the absolute maximum and minimum on \([-2,3]\).
solutionSteps: Critical points satisfy \(3x^2-3=0\), so \(x=\pm1\).
Compare all values.
hints: Check both critical points and endpoints.

questions.question: Determine intervals of concavity and inflection points.
solutionSteps: Compute \(f''(x)=12x(x-2)\).
Make a sign chart around \(x=0\) and \(x=2\).
Evaluate the function at both sign-change locations.
hints: Concavity is determined by the sign of the second derivative.

questions.question: A rectangle has perimeter 40. Find the dimensions that maximize its area.
solutionSteps: Write \(y=20-x\).
hints: Express the area using one variable.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Integrate term by term.
Add the constant \(C\).
hints: Increase each power by one and divide by the new power.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Apply the power rule to each term.
hints: Use the integration power rule for both negative and fractional exponents.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Use \(\int\frac1x\,dx=\ln|x|+C\).
Multiply by 4.
hints: The \(1/x\) case is the logarithmic exception to the power rule.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Reverse the chain rule.
Divide by the derivative \(2\) of the exponent.
hints: Differentiate your result to check the factor of \(1/2\).

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Use \(\int a^x\,dx=\frac{a^x}{\ln a}+C\).
hints: For base \(a\neq e\), divide by \(\ln a\).

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: \(\int\cos x\,dx=\sin x\).
\(\int-2\sin x\,dx=2\cos x\).
hints: Check signs by differentiating the cosine term.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Use the derivative pair \((\tan x)'=\sec^2x\).
hints: Recall the derivative of tangent.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Match the standard arctangent antiderivative.
hints: Recall \((\arctan x)'=1/(1+x^2)\).

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Match the standard arcsine antiderivative.
hints: Recall the derivative of \(\arcsin x\).

questions.question: Find \(F(x)\) given the derivative and initial value.
solutionSteps: Integrate: \(F(x)=x^3-2x^2+x+C\).
Use \(F(0)=7\) to obtain \(C=7\).
hints: Integrate first, then use the initial condition.

questions.question: Evaluate the definite integral.
solutionSteps: An antiderivative is \(x^3+x\).
hints: Use the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.

questions.question: Evaluate the definite integral.
solutionSteps: The odd term \(x^3\) integrates to zero on \([-1,1]\).
Evaluate the remaining even terms to get \(\frac43-8=-\frac{20}{3}\).
hints: Use symmetry to simplify the odd term.

questions.question: Evaluate the definite integral.
solutionSteps: An antiderivative is \(-\cos x\).
Evaluate \([-\cos x]_0^\pi=2\).
hints: Evaluate the antiderivative at both endpoints.

questions.question: Evaluate the definite integral.
solutionSteps: Use \(\cos^2x=\frac{1+\cos2x}{2}\).
Integrate from \(0\) to \(\pi/2\); the sine term vanishes.
hints: Use the cosine power-reduction identity.

questions.question: Evaluate the definite integral.
solutionSteps: Use \([\ln x]_1^e=\ln e-\ln1=1\).
hints: The antiderivative is \(\ln x\) on this positive interval.

questions.question: Find the average value on \([0,3]\).
solutionSteps: Use \(f_{\mathrm{avg}}=\frac{1}{3-0}\int_0^3x^2\,dx\).
The integral is \(9\), so the average is \(3\).
hints: Divide the definite integral by the interval length.

questions.question: Find the area between the curves on \([0,1]\).
solutionSteps: Compute \(\int_0^1(x-x^2)\,dx=\frac12-\frac13=\frac16\).
hints: Integrate top curve minus bottom curve.

questions.question: Find the total area between the curve and the \(x\)-axis on \([-3,3]\).
solutionSteps: The zeros are \(x=\pm2\).
Use symmetry and split where the function changes sign.
hints: Total area uses absolute value, so split at the intercepts.

questions.question: Find \(F'(x)\).
solutionSteps: Apply the Fundamental Theorem with upper limit \(x^2\).
Evaluate the integrand at \(t=x^2\) and multiply by \(2x\).
hints: Use the chain rule for the variable upper limit.

questions.question: Find \(G'(x)\).
solutionSteps: Use \(d/dx\int_{a(x)}^{b(x)}f(t)\,dt=f(b)b'-f(a)a'\).
Substitute \(a=x\) and \(b=2x\).
hints: Differentiate both variable limits.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Integrate \(\int u^4\,du\).
hints: The derivative of the inner expression is already present.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: The integral becomes \(\frac12\int u^{1/2}\,du\).

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Integrate \(1/u\).
hints: The numerator is the derivative of the denominator.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Divide by 4.
hints: Reverse the chain rule.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Let \(u=5x\).
Integrate sine and include the factor \(1/5\).
hints: Differentiate the proposed result to check the sign.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Let \(u=\cos x\), so \(du=-\sin x\,dx\).
Integrate \(-\int u^3\,du\).
hints: Use the factor \(\sin x\,dx\) for substitution.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Let \(u=\ln x\), so \(du=dx/x\).
Integrate \(\int u\,du\).
hints: The derivative of \(\ln x\) is \(1/x\).

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Integrate \(\frac12\int u^{-2}\,du\).
hints: Rewrite the denominator using a negative exponent after substitution.

questions.question: Evaluate the definite integral.
solutionSteps: Let \(u=x^2\), so \(du=2x\,dx\).
Change the bounds from \(x=0,1\) to \(u=0,1\).
Evaluate \(\frac12\int_0^1e^u\,du\).
hints: Change the limits when using substitution in a definite integral.

questions.question: Evaluate the definite integral.
solutionSteps: Let \(u=\tan x\), so \(du=\sec^2x\,dx\).
The new bounds are \(0\) and \(1\).
Evaluate \(\int_0^1u\,du\).
hints: Use \(u=\tan x\).

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Choose \(u=x\) and \(dv=e^x\,dx\).
Then \(\int u\,dv=uv-\int v\,du=xe^x-e^x+C\).
hints: Let the algebraic factor be \(u\).

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Choose \(u=x\), \(dv=\sin x\,dx\).
Then \(v=-\cos x\) and apply integration by parts.
hints: Take \(u=x\) so its derivative becomes simpler.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Choose \(u=\ln x\) and \(dv=x^2\,dx\).
Then \(du=dx/x\) and \(v=x^3/3\).
Apply integration by parts and simplify.
hints: Choose the logarithm as \(u\).

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Apply integration by parts twice.
After the second application, the original integral reappears.
Solve algebraically for the integral.
hints: Two rounds of integration by parts are needed.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Write the integral as \(\int1\cdot\ln x\,dx\).
Choose \(u=\ln x\) and \(dv=dx\).
hints: Treat the integrand as a product with 1.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Use \(\sin^2x=\frac{1-\cos2x}{2}\).
Integrate term by term.
hints: Use a power-reduction identity.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Write \(\sin^3x=\sin x(1-\cos^2x)\).
Let \(u=\cos x\), so \(du=-\sin x\,dx\).
hints: Save one sine factor and convert the rest using \(1-\cos^2x\).

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Write \(\cos^3x=\cos x(1-\sin^2x)\).
Let \(u=\sin x\), so \(du=\cos x\,dx\).
Integrate \(u^2-u^4\).
hints: Save one cosine factor because its power is odd.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Use \(\tan^2x=\sec^2x-1\).
Integrate term by term.
hints: Use the Pythagorean identity for tangent and secant.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Use integration by parts with \(u=\sec x\) and \(dv=\sec^2x\,dx\).
Rewrite \(\tan^2x=\sec^2x-1\).
The original integral reappears; solve for it.
hints: This standard integral requires integration by parts.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Decompose as \(\frac12\frac1{x-1}-\frac12\frac1{x+1}\).
Integrate both logarithmic terms.
hints: Factor the denominator first.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Factor the denominator as \((x+1)(x+2)\).
Decompose into \(\frac2{x+1}+\frac1{x+2}\).
Integrate term by term.
hints: Use partial fractions after factoring the quadratic.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Decompose as \(-\frac1x+\frac1{x-1}+\frac1{x+1}\).
Integrate each term.
hints: Use one partial-fraction term for each linear factor.

questions.question: Determine convergence and evaluate if convergent.
solutionSteps: Replace infinity with \(b\) and integrate \(x^{-2}\).
hints: Use a limit at the infinite endpoint.

questions.question: Determine whether the integral converges.
solutionSteps: Evaluate \(\lim_{b\to\infty}[\ln x]_1^b\).
Since \(\ln b\to\infty\), the integral diverges.
hints: The \(p\)-integral with \(p=1\) diverges.

questions.question: Evaluate the improper integral.
solutionSteps: Write the lower endpoint as a limit: \(\lim_{a\to0^+}\int_a^1x^{-1/2}\,dx\).
Evaluate \([2\sqrt{x}]_a^1\) and take the limit.
hints: The integrand is unbounded at zero, so use a limit.

questions.question: Find the volume formed by rotating the region under the curve on \([0,2]\) about the \(x\)-axis.
solutionSteps: Use the disk method: \(V=\pi\int_0^2(x^2)^2\,dx\).
Evaluate \(\pi[x^5/5]_0^2\).
hints: The disk radius is \(y=x^2\).

questions.question: Find the volume formed by rotating the region bounded by the curves about the \(y\)-axis.
solutionSteps: The curves intersect at \(x=0,1\).
Use cylindrical shells: \(V=2\pi\int_0^1x(x-x^2)\,dx\).
Evaluate to obtain \(\pi/6\).
hints: Shell radius is \(x\), and shell height is \(x-x^2\).

questions.question: Find the arc length on \([0,3]\).
solutionSteps: Compute \(y'=\sqrt{x}\).
Use \(L=\int_0^3\sqrt{1+(y')^2}\,dx=\int_0^3\sqrt{1+x}\,dx\).
Evaluate to obtain \(14/3\).
hints: Start with the arc-length formula.

questions.question: Find displacement and total distance traveled from \(t=0\) to \(t=5\).
solutionSteps: An antiderivative is \(F(t)=t^3/3-2t^2+3t\).
Displacement is \(F(5)-F(0)=20/3\).
Velocity changes sign at \(t=1,3\).
Sum the absolute changes on \([0,1]\), \([1,3]\), and \([3,5]\) to get \(28/3\).
hints: For total distance, split at every zero of velocity.

questions.question: Compute the vector sum.
solutionSteps: Add corresponding components: \(2+5=7\), \(-1+3=2\), and \(4-2=2\).
hints: Add vectors component by component.

questions.question: Compute the vector expression.
solutionSteps: Scale the vectors to obtain \([3,-6,15]^T\) and \([8,0,-2]^T\).
Subtract corresponding components.
hints: Perform scalar multiplication before subtraction.

questions.question: Find the magnitude of the vector.
hints: Square each component, add, and take the square root.

questions.question: Find a unit vector in the given direction.
solutionSteps: The norm is \(\sqrt{4+1+4}=3\).
Divide every component by 3.
hints: A unit vector is \(\mathbf v/\|\mathbf v\|\).

questions.question: Compute the dot product.
hints: Multiply corresponding components and add.

questions.question: Find the angle between the vectors.
solutionSteps: \(\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v=1\), and both norms are \(\sqrt2\).
Thus \(\cos\theta=1/2\), so \(\theta=\pi/3\).
hints: Use \(\cos\theta=(\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v)/(\|\mathbf u\|\|\mathbf v\|)\).

questions.question: Determine whether the vectors are parallel.
questions.answer: \text{Parallel; the second vector is }-2\text{ times the first}
hints: Check whether one vector is a scalar multiple of the other.

questions.question: Find the projection of \(\mathbf u\) onto \(\mathbf v\).
solutionSteps: Use \(\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf u=\frac{\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v}{\mathbf v\cdot\mathbf v}\mathbf v\).
hints: Use the projection formula.

questions.question: Find a vector orthogonal to both vectors.
solutionSteps: Compute the cross product \(\mathbf u\times\mathbf v\).
The result is \((2,1,-1)\), which has zero dot product with each original vector.
hints: Use the cross product in \(\mathbb R^3\).

questions.question: Find the area of the parallelogram spanned by the vectors.
solutionSteps: \(\mathbf u\times\mathbf v=(5,5,-5)\).
The area is its norm: \(\sqrt{25+25+25}=5\sqrt3\).
hints: Parallelogram area equals the norm of the cross product.

questions.question: Solve the system.
solutionSteps: Add the equations to get \(3x=9\).
Then \(x=3\), and substitution gives \(y=1\).
hints: Elimination is especially quick here.

questions.question: Solve the system.
solutionSteps: Adding the first two equations gives \(3x+y+2z=12\).
The third equation requires the same left side to equal 10.
This contradiction means the system is inconsistent.
hints: Compare the third equation with the sum of the first two.

questions.question: Row-reduce the augmented matrix.
solutionSteps: Apply elementary row operations to create pivots.
The final row represents \(0=1\), so the corresponding system is inconsistent.
hints: A pivot in the augmented column signals a contradiction.

questions.question: Classify the system.
solutionSteps: The first and third equations have identical left sides but different constants.
Therefore the system is inconsistent.
hints: Look for contradictory equations before row-reducing.

questions.question: Find all values of \(a\) for which the system has a unique solution.
solutionSteps: The coefficient determinant is \(1-a^2\).
A unique solution exists when \(1-a^2\ne0\).
Thus \(a\ne\pm1\).
hints: A square system has a unique solution when its coefficient determinant is nonzero.

questions.question: Express the solution set in parametric vector form.
solutionSteps: Let \(y=s\) and \(z=t\).
Then \(x=-2s+t\).
Collect the coefficients of \(s\) and \(t\).
hints: Choose the free variables as parameters.

questions.question: Determine whether \(\mathbf b\) lies in the given span.
solutionSteps: Set \(c_1\mathbf v_1+c_2\mathbf v_2=\mathbf b\).
The first two components give \(c_1=4\) and \(c_2=1\).
The third component checks: \(8-1=7\).
hints: Solve for the coefficients using the easiest components first.

questions.question: Find a basis for the solution space.
solutionSteps: Let \(y=s\) and \(z=t\), so \(x=-2s+3t\).
Write the general solution as a linear combination of two vectors.
hints: Use the free variables to generate basis vectors.

questions.question: Find the rank and nullity.
solutionSteps: Row reduction produces two pivot rows.
Thus the rank is 2.
With three columns, rank-nullity gives nullity \(3-2=1\).
hints: Count pivots, then use rank plus nullity equals the number of columns.

questions.question: Determine whether the vectors are linearly independent.
solutionSteps: The second vector is \(2\) times the first.
Therefore a nontrivial linear relation exists.
hints: A set is dependent if one vector is a scalar multiple of another.

questions.question: Compute the matrix sum.
solutionSteps: Add corresponding entries.
hints: Matrix addition is entrywise.

questions.question: Compute the matrix product.
solutionSteps: Take each row of the first matrix dotted with each column of the second matrix.
hints: Use row-by-column multiplication.

questions.question: Compute \(AB\) and \(BA\).
solutionSteps: Multiply row by column to find \(AB\).
Reverse the order and repeat for \(BA\).
The results differ, illustrating that matrix multiplication is not generally commutative.
hints: Keep the multiplication order explicit.

questions.question: Compute \(A^2\).
solutionSteps: Multiply \(A\) by itself.
hints: Matrix squaring means matrix multiplication, not entrywise squaring.

questions.question: Find the transpose.
solutionSteps: Turn the rows of \(A\) into the columns of \(A^T\).
hints: Swap row and column indices.

questions.question: Find the inverse.
solutionSteps: The determinant is \(2(3)-1(5)=1\).
Use \(A^{-1}=\frac1{\det A}\begin{bmatrix}d&-b\\-c&a\end{bmatrix}\).
hints: Use the \(2\times2\) inverse formula.

questions.question: Use a matrix inverse to solve the system.
solutionSteps: Write \(A\mathbf x=\mathbf b\).
Compute \(A^{-1}\mathbf b\), where \(A=\begin{bmatrix}2&3\\1&2\end{bmatrix}\).
The result is \([2,1]^T\).
hints: Solve \(\mathbf x=A^{-1}\mathbf b\).

questions.question: Determine whether the matrix is invertible.
solutionSteps: A matrix with zero determinant is singular.
hints: Check the determinant.

questions.question: Solve for \(X\) in \(AX=B\).
solutionSteps: Multiply by \(A^{-1}\) on the left: \(X=A^{-1}B\).
\(A^{-1}=\begin{bmatrix}1&-1/2\\0&1/2\end{bmatrix}\).
Perform the multiplication.
hints: Preserve multiplication order when isolating \(X\).

questions.question: Verify \((AB)^{-1}=B^{-1}A^{-1}\).
solutionSteps: Compute \(AB=\begin{bmatrix}2&3\\0&3\end{bmatrix}\) and invert it.
Compute \(A^{-1}\) and \(B^{-1}\), then multiply in the order \(B^{-1}A^{-1}\).
Both results match.
hints: The inverse reverses the multiplication order.

questions.question: Compute the determinant.
hints: For a \(2\times2\) matrix, use \(ad-bc\).

questions.question: Compute the determinant.
solutionSteps: Expand along the first row or use row reduction.
The cofactor calculation gives \(24+10-12=22\).
hints: Choose a convenient determinant method.

questions.question: Use cofactor expansion to compute the determinant.
solutionSteps: Expand along the first row.
This equals \(-26+7=-19\).
hints: The zero in the first row eliminates one cofactor term.

questions.question: Find all values of \(k\) for which the matrix is singular.
solutionSteps: Set the determinant \(k^2-6\) equal to zero.
Solve \(k^2=6\).
hints: Singular means determinant zero.

questions.question: Determine whether the polynomials are linearly independent in \(P_2\).
solutionSteps: Represent the polynomials by coefficient vectors \((1,1,0)\), \((0,1,1)\), and \((1,0,1)\).
The determinant of the coefficient matrix is nonzero.
Therefore the set is linearly independent.
hints: Convert each polynomial to its coefficient vector.

questions.question: Find a basis and the dimension of the span.
solutionSteps: The third vector equals the sum of the first two.
The first two are not scalar multiples, so they are independent.
hints: Remove vectors that are combinations of earlier vectors.

questions.question: Find bases for the column space and null space.
solutionSteps: Row reduction shows pivots in columns 1 and 3.
Use the corresponding original columns for a column-space basis.
Solving \(A\mathbf x=0\) gives \(\mathbf x=t(-2,1,0)\).
hints: Use pivot columns from the original matrix, not the reduced matrix.

questions.question: Find the standard matrix of the transformation.
solutionSteps: Use these vectors as the matrix columns.
hints: The standard matrix columns are the images of the standard basis vectors.

questions.question: Determine the kernel and range.
solutionSteps: For the kernel, solve \(x+y=0\) and \(y+z=0\), giving \((-y,y,-y)\).
The transformation matrix has two pivot rows, so its range is all of \(\mathbb R^2\).
hints: Solve \(T(\mathbf x)=\mathbf0\) for the kernel.

questions.question: Determine whether the transformation is linear.
solutionSteps: A linear transformation must satisfy \(T(0,0)=(0,0)\).
Here \(T(0,0)=(1,0)\), so the condition fails.
hints: Check what the transformation does to the zero vector.

questions.question: Find the eigenvalues.
solutionSteps: For a diagonal matrix, the eigenvalues are its diagonal entries.
hints: Read the eigenvalues from the diagonal.

questions.question: Find the eigenvalues and eigenvectors.
solutionSteps: Compute \(\det(A-\lambda I)=(2-\lambda)^2-1\).
The roots are \(\lambda=3,1\).
Solve each null-space equation to obtain the eigenvectors.
hints: Factor the characteristic polynomial as \((\lambda-3)(\lambda-1)\).

questions.question: Find the characteristic polynomial.
solutionSteps: Compute \(\det(\lambda I-A)=(\lambda-1)(\lambda-4)-6\).
Expand and simplify.
hints: Use \(p(\lambda)=\det(\lambda I-A)\).

questions.question: Determine whether the matrix is diagonalizable.
solutionSteps: The only eigenvalue is \(4\), with algebraic multiplicity 2.
Solving \((A-4I)\mathbf v=0\) gives a one-dimensional eigenspace.
There are not enough independent eigenvectors.
hints: Compare algebraic and geometric multiplicities.

questions.question: Diagonalize the matrix if possible.
solutionSteps: The matrix is already diagonal.
Choose the standard basis eigenvectors, so \(P=I\) and \(D=A\).
hints: A diagonal matrix is already diagonalized.

questions.question: Use diagonalization to compute \(A^5\).
solutionSteps: The eigenvalues are \(3\) and \(1\), with eigenvectors \((1,1)\) and \((1,-1)\).
Equivalently, \(A^n=\frac12\begin{bmatrix}3^n+1&3^n-1\\3^n-1&3^n+1\end{bmatrix}\).
hints: Raise the diagonal eigenvalue matrix to the fifth power.

questions.question: Apply the Gram-Schmidt process.
solutionSteps: Normalize \(\mathbf v_1\) to obtain \(\mathbf e_1\).
Subtract the projection of \(\mathbf v_2\) onto \(\mathbf v_1\), giving a vector proportional to \((1,-1,2)\).
Normalize the second vector.
hints: Orthogonalize first, then normalize.

questions.question: Find an orthonormal basis for the plane.
solutionSteps: Choose two independent vectors in the plane, such as \((1,-1,0)\) and \((1,1,-2)\).
They are already orthogonal.
Normalize both vectors.
hints: Pick vectors perpendicular to the normal \((1,1,1)\).

questions.question: Find the least-squares solution.
solutionSteps: Form the normal equations \(A^TA\hat{\mathbf x}=A^T\mathbf b\).
They become \(\begin{bmatrix}3&6\\6&14\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}a\\b\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}5\\11\end{bmatrix}\).
Solving gives \(a=2/3\) and \(b=1/2\).
hints: Use the normal equations.

questions.question: Determine whether \(Q\) is orthogonal and interpret its geometric effect.
solutionSteps: Compute \(Q^TQ=I\), so \(Q\) is orthogonal.
Compare with \(\begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta&-\sin\theta\\\sin\theta&\cos\theta\end{bmatrix}\).
Here \(\cos\theta=\sin\theta=1/\sqrt2\), so \(\theta=\pi/4\).
hints: Check \(Q^TQ\) and compare with the standard rotation matrix.

questions.question: A fair six-sided die is rolled. Write the probability formula for rolling an even number. Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: Identify the sample space \(\Omega\) and the event \(E\).
For equally likely outcomes, use \(P(E)=|E|/|\Omega|\).
hints: Use favorable outcomes divided by total outcomes.

questions.question: Two fair coins are tossed. Write a formula for the probability of getting exactly one head. Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: Recognize a binomial experiment with 2 independent trials.
Use \(P(X=k)=\binom nkp^k(1-p)^{n-k}\).
hints: Use the binomial formula with \(n=2\) and \(k=1\).

questions.question: A card is drawn from a standard 52-card deck. Write a formula for the probability that it is not a heart. Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: The desired event is the complement of drawing a heart.
hints: Use the complement rule.

questions.question: A fair die is rolled. Let \(A\) be the event of rolling an even number and \(B\) the event of rolling a number greater than 4. Write the formula for \(P(A\cup B)\). Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: The events overlap because 6 belongs to both \(A\) and \(B\).
Use the addition rule for a union.
hints: Subtract the overlap so it is not counted twice.

questions.question: Five different books are arranged on a shelf. Write the counting formula for the number of possible arrangements. Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: All 5 objects are distinct and order matters.
Use the permutation count \(n!\).
hints: Arranging all distinct objects uses a factorial.

questions.question: A 3-person committee is selected from 8 people. Write the counting formula. Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: Order does not matter for a committee.
Use the combination formula \(\binom nr\).
hints: Use combinations, not permutations.

questions.question: Write the counting formula for the number of distinct arrangements of the letters in LEVEL. Do not evaluate it.
questions.expression: 5\text{ letters total; }L\text{ repeats twice and }E\text{ repeats twice}
solutionSteps: Begin with \(5!\) arrangements.
Divide by the factorial of each repeated-letter count.
hints: Use the repeated-permutation formula.

questions.question: Write the counting expression for the number of 4-digit numbers that can be formed from the digits 0 through 9 without repetition. Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: There are 9 choices for the first digit because zero is excluded.
Then use the multiplication rule for the remaining positions.
hints: Treat the first digit separately.

questions.question: A group contains 6 women and 5 men. Write the counting formula for a 4-person committee containing exactly 2 women. Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: Choose the women and men independently.
Multiply the two combination counts.
hints: Use one combination for each group.

questions.question: Write the counting formula for the number of binary strings of length 8 containing exactly three 1s. Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: Only the positions of the 1s need to be chosen.
The remaining positions are automatically 0s.
hints: Choose 3 positions from 8.

questions.question: A card is drawn from a standard deck. Given that it is a face card, write the conditional-probability formula for the probability that it is a king. Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: Restrict the sample space to face cards.
Use \(P(A\mid B)=P(A\cap B)/P(B)\).
hints: The condition becomes the denominator event.

questions.question: Two fair dice are rolled. Given that the sum is 8, write the conditional-probability formula for the event that at least one die shows 3. Do not evaluate it.
questions.expression: S=\{\text{sum is }8\},\quad A=\{\text{at least one die is }3\}
solutionSteps: Condition on the five ordered outcomes whose sum is 8.
Count the outcomes in that restricted space that contain a 3.
hints: Build the conditional sample space first.

questions.question: Events \(A\) and \(B\) satisfy \(P(A)=0.4\), \(P(B)=0.5\), and \(P(A\cap B)=0.2\). Write the criterion used to test whether they are independent.
questions.answer: A\text{ and }B\text{ are independent iff }P(A\cap B)=P(A)P(B)
solutionSteps: Independence is checked by comparing the intersection probability with the product of the marginal probabilities.
hints: Use the product criterion for independence.

questions.question: A fair coin is tossed three times. Let \(A\) be the event that the first toss is heads and \(B\) the event that exactly two heads occur. Write a formula for \(P(A\cap B)\). Do not evaluate it.
questions.expression: A=\{\text{first toss is H}\},\quad B=\{\text{exactly two H}\}
solutionSteps: List the outcomes satisfying both events.
Divide the number of favorable equally likely sequences by \(2^3\).
hints: The intersection must satisfy both conditions simultaneously.

questions.question: Machine 1 makes 60% of a factory's items and has a 2% defect rate. Machine 2 makes 40% and has a 5% defect rate. Write the formula for the probability that a randomly selected item is defective. Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: The machine events partition the production process.
Apply the law of total probability across the two machines.
hints: Weight each conditional defect probability by the machine share.

questions.question: Machine 1 makes 60% of a factory's items and has a 2% defect rate. Machine 2 makes 40% and has a 5% defect rate. Write the formula for the probability that a defective item came from Machine 2. Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: Use Bayes' theorem with \(M_2\) as the target event and \(D\) as the observed event.
Expand \(P(D)\) using the law of total probability.
hints: The denominator is the total probability of a defect.

questions.question: A disease affects 1% of a population. A test has 95% sensitivity and 90% specificity. Write the Bayes' theorem formula for the probability that a person who tests positive has the disease. Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: Convert specificity into the false-positive probability \(P(+\mid D^c)\).
Use Bayes' theorem with a total-probability denominator.
hints: Specificity determines the false-positive rate by complement.

questions.question: A bag contains 4 red and 6 blue balls. Two balls are drawn without replacement. Write a formula for the probability that both are red. Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: Use the multiplication rule for sequential dependent events.
Update both the numerator and denominator after the first red ball is removed.
hints: Without replacement, the second probability changes.

questions.question: A bag contains 4 red and 6 blue balls. Two balls are drawn without replacement. Write a formula for the probability that exactly one ball is red. Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: Exactly one red can occur in two disjoint orders: red-blue or blue-red.
Add the probabilities of those two paths.
hints: Consider both possible orders.

questions.question: Assuming 365 equally likely birthdays and no leap day, write a formula for the probability that at least two people in a group of 23 share a birthday. Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: Use the complement event that all birthdays are distinct.
Multiply the successive probabilities of avoiding a repeated birthday.
hints: The complement is easier to model.

questions.question: A random variable \(X\) has \(P(X=0)=0.2\), \(P(X=1)=0.5\), and \(P(X=2)=0.3\). Write the formula for \(E[X]\). Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: Expected value is a probability-weighted average.
Multiply each possible value by its probability and sum.
hints: Use \(E[X]=\sum_xxP(X=x)\).

questions.question: A random variable \(X\) has \(P(X=0)=0.2\), \(P(X=1)=0.5\), and \(P(X=2)=0.3\). Write a formula for \(\operatorname{Var}(X)\). Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: Use the shortcut formula for variance.
Compute \(E[X]\) and \(E[X^2]\) from the same probability mass function.
hints: Variance compares \(E[X^2]\) with \(E[X]^2\).

questions.question: A game pays $10 with probability 0.2 and loses $3 with probability 0.8. Write the expected-payoff formula. Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: Treat the loss as a negative payoff.
Form the probability-weighted sum of all possible payoffs.
hints: Expected payoff is a weighted average.

questions.question: A fair coin is tossed 10 times. Write the binomial formula for the probability of exactly 6 heads. Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: Recognize 10 independent Bernoulli trials.
Substitute \(n=10\), \(k=6\), and \(p=1/2\) into the binomial formula.
hints: Use \(\binom nkp^k(1-p)^{n-k}\).

questions.question: A basketball player makes a free throw with probability 0.8. In 5 independent attempts, write the formula for the probability of making at least 4 shots. Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: 'At least 4' means the disjoint cases \(X=4\) and \(X=5\).
Add the two binomial probabilities.
hints: Translate 'at least 4' into a sum of cases.

questions.question: For \(X\sim\operatorname{Binomial}(20,0.3)\), write the formulas for \(E[X]\) and \(\operatorname{Var}(X)\).
solutionSteps: Use the standard mean and variance formulas for a binomial random variable.
hints: Identify \(n\) and \(p\).

questions.question: A fair die is rolled repeatedly until a 6 appears. Write the geometric probability formula for the first 6 occurring on the fourth roll. Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: The first three trials must fail and the fourth must succeed.
hints: Geometric probability is failures followed by one success.

questions.question: A trial succeeds with probability 0.2. Write the formula for the expected number of trials until the first success.
solutionSteps: Use the standard mean formula for a geometric random variable counting trials until the first success.
hints: The geometric mean is the reciprocal of the success probability.

questions.question: Calls arrive at an average rate of 3 per hour. Write the Poisson formula for the probability of exactly 5 calls in one hour. Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: Use the Poisson probability mass function \(P(X=k)=e^{-\lambda}\lambda^k/k!\).
hints: Identify the rate parameter \(\lambda\).

questions.question: Calls arrive at an average rate of 3 per hour. Write the Poisson formula for the probability of no calls in a 30-minute interval. Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: Scale the hourly rate to a half-hour interval.
Then use the Poisson probability mass function with \(k=0\).
hints: Adjust the rate to the time interval first.

questions.question: Let \(X\) have density \(f(x)=2x\) for \(0\le x\le1\). Write the integral for \(P(X\le0.5)\). Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: For a continuous random variable, probability is area under the density.
Integrate over the event interval.
hints: Use the density as the integrand.

questions.question: Let \(f(x)=cx^2\) for \(0\le x\le2\), and \(f(x)=0\) otherwise. Write the normalization equation used to determine \(c\).
solutionSteps: A probability density must integrate to 1 over its full support.
hints: Set the total area under the density equal to 1.

questions.question: Let \(X\) have density \(f(x)=\frac38x^2\) on \(0\le x\le2\). Write the integral formula for \(E[X]\). Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: For a continuous random variable, use \(E[X]=\int x f(x)\,dx\).
hints: Multiply the density by \(x\) before integrating.

questions.question: If \(X\sim\operatorname{Uniform}(2,8)\), write the formula for \(P(3\le X\le5)\). Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: For a continuous uniform distribution, probability is interval length divided by total length.
hints: Compare the desired interval length with the full support length.

questions.question: If \(X\sim\operatorname{Uniform}(2,8)\), write the formulas for \(E[X]\) and \(\operatorname{Var}(X)\).
solutionSteps: Use the standard continuous-uniform mean and variance formulas.
hints: Identify the lower and upper endpoints.

questions.question: The waiting time \(X\) is exponential with rate \(\lambda=0.5\) per minute. Write the formula for \(P(X>4)\). Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: Use the survival function of an exponential random variable.
hints: The exponential tail probability is \(e^{-\lambda t}\).

questions.question: For an exponential random variable with rate \(\lambda=0.5\), write the formulas for the expected value and variance.
solutionSteps: Use the standard exponential-distribution formulas.
hints: Both formulas are powers of the reciprocal rate.

questions.question: Let \(X\sim N(100,15^2)\). Write the standardization formula used to convert \(X=130\) to a \(z\)-score. Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: Subtract the mean from the observation and divide by the standard deviation.
hints: Use \(z=(x-\mu)/\sigma\).

questions.question: Let \(Z\sim N(0,1)\). Write the notation for the probability \(P(Z\le1.96)\) using the standard normal cumulative distribution function.
solutionSteps: The standard normal cumulative distribution function is denoted by \(\Phi\).
hints: Express the probability with \(\Phi\).

questions.question: Let \(X\sim N(50,10^2)\). Write the standardized probability expression for \(P(40\le X\le60)\). Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: Standardize both endpoints using the same mean and standard deviation.
hints: Transform both bounds to \(z\)-scores.

questions.question: A joint distribution satisfies \(P(X=0,Y=0)=0.2\), \(P(0,1)=0.3\), \(P(1,0)=0.1\), and \(P(1,1)=0.4\). Write the formula for the marginal probability \(P(X=1)\). Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: Marginalize over all possible values of \(Y\).
hints: Sum the joint probabilities across the relevant row.

questions.question: A joint distribution satisfies \(P(X=0,Y=0)=0.2\), \(P(0,1)=0.3\), \(P(1,0)=0.1\), and \(P(1,1)=0.4\). Write the criterion used to test whether \(X\) and \(Y\) are independent.
questions.answer: X\text{ and }Y\text{ are independent iff }P(X=x,Y=y)=P(X=x)P(Y=y)\text{ for all }x,y
solutionSteps: Independence requires every joint probability to factor into the corresponding marginals.
hints: Check the factorization for all possible pairs.

questions.question: Suppose \(E[X]=2\), \(E[Y]=3\), and \(E[XY]=8\). Write the covariance formula. Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: Use the covariance shortcut formula.
hints: Covariance compares the joint expectation with the product of the means.

questions.question: Suppose \(E[X]=4\) and \(E[Y]=-1\). Write the formula for \(E[3X-2Y+5]\). Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: Apply linearity of expectation term by term.
Independence is not required.
hints: Constants pass through expectation linearly.

questions.question: Suppose \(\operatorname{Var}(X)=9\). Write the formula for \(\operatorname{Var}(4X-7)\). Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: Adding a constant does not affect variance.
Multiplying by a constant scales variance by the square of that constant.
hints: Use \(\operatorname{Var}(aX+b)=a^2\operatorname{Var}(X)\).

questions.question: A nonnegative random variable has \(E[X]=10\). Write Markov's inequality for \(P(X\ge25)\). Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: Markov's inequality applies to nonnegative random variables.
Substitute the threshold into \(P(X\ge a)\le E[X]/a\).
hints: The threshold appears in the denominator.

questions.question: A random variable has mean 50 and standard deviation 4. Write Chebyshev's inequality for \(P(|X-50|\ge12)\). Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: Express the distance from the mean as a multiple of the standard deviation.
Apply \(P(|X-\mu|\ge k\sigma)\le1/k^2\).
hints: First identify \(k\).

questions.question: Write the mathematical statement describing the law of large numbers for the sample mean of independent observations with finite mean \(\mu\).
solutionSteps: The sample mean converges in probability to the population mean.
hints: Use convergence in probability notation.

questions.question: A population has mean 80 and standard deviation 12. For samples of size 36, write the central-limit-theorem standardization for \(P(\bar X>83)\). Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: The sample mean has approximate standard error \(\sigma/\sqrt n\).
Standardize the sample-mean threshold using that standard error.
hints: Use \(\sigma/\sqrt n\), not \(\sigma\).

questions.question: For \(X\sim\operatorname{Binomial}(100,0.5)\), write the normal approximation with continuity correction for \(P(X\le55)\). Do not evaluate it.
solutionSteps: Approximate the binomial distribution with a normal distribution having the same mean and variance.
Use 55.5 for the continuity correction.
hints: For a 'less than or equal to' event, shift the boundary up by 0.5.

questions.question: A survey records each participant's favorite color. Classify this variable as categorical or quantitative.
solutionSteps: Favorite color records a label rather than a numerical measurement.
Therefore, the variable is categorical.
hints: Ask whether arithmetic operations on the values would be meaningful.

questions.question: A study records the number of text messages sent by each participant in one day. Classify this variable as discrete or continuous.
solutionSteps: The variable is numerical.
It takes countable whole-number values, so it is discrete.
hints: Counts are typically discrete.

questions.question: Classify temperature measured in degrees Celsius as nominal, ordinal, interval, or ratio.
solutionSteps: Differences in Celsius temperature are meaningful.
Zero degrees Celsius is not a true absence of temperature, so the scale is interval rather than ratio.
hints: A ratio scale requires a meaningful zero.

questions.question: Write the formula for the sample mean of observations \(x_1,\dots,x_n\).
solutionSteps: Add all observations.
Divide the total by the number of observations.
hints: The sample mean is the arithmetic average.

questions.question: Describe how to find the median of a data set containing an even number \(n\) of observations.
solutionSteps: First sort the observations.
For even \(n\), average the two middle ordered values.
hints: The median depends on position after sorting.

questions.question: Write the formula for the sample variance.
solutionSteps: Measure each observation\'s deviation from the sample mean.
Square the deviations and divide their sum by \(n-1\).
hints: Use n-1, not n, for sample variance.

questions.question: Write the relationship between sample variance and sample standard deviation.
solutionSteps: Standard deviation is defined as the positive square root of variance.
hints: Variance is measured in squared units; standard deviation returns to the original units.

questions.question: Write the formula for the interquartile range.
solutionSteps: The interquartile range measures the spread of the middle 50 percent of the data.
Subtract the first quartile from the third quartile.
hints: The IQR uses quartiles rather than the minimum and maximum.

questions.question: Write the standard IQR-based lower and upper fences used to identify potential outliers.
solutionSteps: Compute the interquartile range.
Extend 1.5 IQRs below \(Q_1\) and above \(Q_3\).
hints: Potential outliers lie outside the two fences.

questions.question: Write the formula for the \(z\)-score of an observation \(x\) from a population with mean \(\mu\) and standard deviation \(\sigma\).
solutionSteps: Subtract the population mean from the observation.
Divide by the population standard deviation.
hints: A z-score measures distance from the mean in standard deviations.

questions.question: Which graph is generally most appropriate for displaying the distribution of one quantitative variable?
solutionSteps: A histogram groups quantitative observations into intervals.
It displays shape, center, spread, and possible outliers.
hints: Bar charts are primarily for categorical variables.

questions.question: Which graph is generally most appropriate for comparing frequencies across categories?
solutionSteps: A bar chart uses one bar for each category.
Bar heights represent counts or proportions.
hints: Categorical values form separate groups rather than numeric intervals.

questions.question: A distribution has a long tail extending to the right. How should its shape be described?
solutionSteps: Skewness is named for the direction of the longer tail.
A long right tail indicates positive skew.
hints: Name the skew by the direction of the tail.

questions.question: For a strongly right-skewed distribution with several large outliers, which measures of center and spread are usually preferred?
solutionSteps: The mean and standard deviation are sensitive to extreme values.
The median and IQR are more resistant to skewness and outliers.
hints: Prefer resistant summaries when outliers are present.

questions.question: A researcher selects every 20th name from an alphabetized list after choosing a random starting point. Identify the sampling method.
solutionSteps: Systematic sampling selects observations at a fixed interval.
A random starting point helps reduce selection bias.
hints: Look for a fixed step size.

questions.question: A population is divided into age groups, and a random sample is taken from each age group. Identify the sampling method.
solutionSteps: The population is divided into meaningful subgroups called strata.
Random observations are selected from each stratum.
hints: Every subgroup contributes observations.

questions.question: A school randomly selects 5 classrooms and surveys every student in those classrooms. Identify the sampling method.
questions.expression: \text{Randomly select classrooms, then survey all students within them}
solutionSteps: The population is divided into naturally occurring groups.
Whole selected groups are included in the sample.
hints: Cluster sampling selects entire groups.

questions.question: An online news site asks visitors to click a link if they want to participate in a political poll. Identify the main source of bias.
solutionSteps: Individuals decide for themselves whether to participate.
People with strong opinions may be overrepresented.
hints: The sample is self-selected.

questions.question: A study observes that people who exercise more tend to report lower stress, but participants were not randomly assigned to exercise levels. What type of study is this?
solutionSteps: The researchers measured existing behavior rather than assigning treatments.
Therefore, the study is observational.
hints: Random assignment distinguishes experiments from observational studies.

questions.question: Why can an observational study usually establish association but not causation?
questions.answer: \text{Potential confounding variables and lack of random assignment prevent a clear causal conclusion}
solutionSteps: Observed groups may differ in ways other than the explanatory variable.
Without random assignment, confounding cannot usually be ruled out.
hints: Association alone does not show that one variable causes another.

questions.question: State the empirical rule for a normally distributed variable.
solutionSteps: The empirical rule summarizes approximate coverage around the mean in a normal distribution.

questions.question: Write the expected value of the sample mean \(\bar X\) for random samples from a population with mean \(\mu\).
solutionSteps: The sample mean is an unbiased estimator of the population mean.
hints: Unbiased means its expected value equals the parameter.

questions.question: Write the standard error of the sample mean when the population standard deviation is \(\sigma\) and the sample size is \(n\).
solutionSteps: The variability of the sample mean decreases as sample size increases.
Divide the population standard deviation by \(\sqrt n\).
hints: Larger samples produce smaller standard errors.

questions.question: State the central limit theorem for the sample mean in conceptual terms.
questions.answer: \text{For sufficiently large }n,\ \bar X\text{ is approximately normal with mean }\mu\text{ and standard error }\sigma/\sqrt n
solutionSteps: The theorem concerns the sampling distribution of the sample mean.
Its approximation improves as sample size grows under standard conditions.
hints: The population itself need not be normal when n is large enough.

questions.question: If the sample size is multiplied by 4, how does the standard error of the sample mean change?
solutionSteps: Replacing \(n\) by \(4n\) gives \(\sigma/\sqrt{4n}\).
Since \(\sqrt{4n}=2\sqrt n\), the standard error is halved.
hints: Standard error changes with the square root of sample size.

questions.question: Write the expected value of the sample proportion \(\hat p\) when the population proportion is \(p\).
solutionSteps: The sample proportion is an unbiased estimator of the population proportion.
hints: The estimator is centered at the true proportion.

questions.question: Write the standard error of the sample proportion \(\hat p\).
solutionSteps: The variance of a Bernoulli outcome is \(p(1-p)\).
Average over \(n\) independent observations.
hints: Use the Bernoulli variance inside the square root.

questions.question: State the usual success-failure condition for using a normal approximation to the sampling distribution of \(\hat p\).
solutionSteps: The expected numbers of successes and failures should both be sufficiently large.
hints: Check both expected counts.

questions.question: When sampling without replacement from a finite population of size \(N\), write the finite population correction factor for the standard error.
solutionSteps: Sampling without replacement reduces variability.
The correction factor adjusts the usual standard error downward.
hints: The factor approaches 1 when the sample is small relative to the population.

questions.question: State the law of large numbers for the sample mean.
solutionSteps: As sample size grows, the sample mean becomes increasingly close to the population mean.
hints: Use convergence in probability notation.

questions.question: Write the general form of a confidence interval.
solutionSteps: A confidence interval is centered at an estimate.
The margin of error is the critical value times the standard error.
hints: Think estimate plus or minus margin of error.

questions.question: Write the \(z\)-interval for a population mean when the population standard deviation \(\sigma\) is known.
solutionSteps: Use the sample mean as the point estimate.
Use \(\sigma/\sqrt n\) as the standard error.
hints: Known sigma leads to a z-critical value.

questions.question: Write the t-interval for a population mean when the population standard deviation is unknown.
solutionSteps: Replace the unknown population standard deviation with the sample standard deviation.
Use a t-critical value with \(n-1\) degrees of freedom.
hints: Unknown sigma leads to a t-interval.

questions.question: Write the confidence interval for a population proportion \(p\).
solutionSteps: Use the sample proportion as the point estimate.
Estimate the standard error using \(\hat p\).
hints: The proportion interval uses the sample proportion inside the standard error.

questions.question: What happens to the width of a confidence interval when the confidence level increases while all other quantities remain fixed?
solutionSteps: A higher confidence level requires a larger critical value.
A larger critical value increases the margin of error.
hints: More confidence requires a broader range.

questions.question: What happens to the margin of error when the sample size increases while all other quantities remain fixed?
solutionSteps: Standard error typically contains a factor of \(1/\sqrt n\).
Therefore, larger samples reduce the margin of error.
hints: Larger samples improve precision.

questions.question: Write the standard structure of a null hypothesis and an alternative hypothesis for testing a population mean against a specific value \(\mu_0\).
solutionSteps: The null hypothesis contains equality.
The alternative reflects the research question and may be two-sided or one-sided.
hints: Equality belongs in H0.

questions.question: Define a p-value in conceptual terms.
questions.answer: \text{The probability, assuming }H_0\text{ is true, of observing a result at least as extreme as the one obtained}
solutionSteps: The p-value is calculated under the null hypothesis.
It measures how unusual the observed evidence would be if the null were true.
hints: A p-value is not the probability that H0 is true.

questions.question: State the usual decision rule comparing a p-value with a significance level \(\alpha\).
solutionSteps: Compare the observed p-value with the chosen significance threshold.
hints: Smaller p-values provide stronger evidence against H0.

questions.question: Define a Type I error.
solutionSteps: A Type I error is a false positive.
Its probability is controlled by the significance level \(\alpha\).
hints: Type I means rejecting a true null.

questions.question: Define a Type II error.
questions.answer: \text{Failing to reject }H_0\text{ when }H_0\text{ is actually false}
solutionSteps: A Type II error is a false negative.
Its probability is commonly denoted by \(\beta\).
hints: Type II means missing a real effect.

questions.question: Write the relationship between statistical power and the Type II error probability \(\beta\).
solutionSteps: Power is the probability of correctly rejecting a false null hypothesis.
hints: Power and Type II error are complements.

questions.question: Write the test statistic for a one-sample t-test of a population mean.
solutionSteps: Subtract the hypothesized mean from the sample mean.
Divide by the estimated standard error.
hints: Use the sample standard deviation because sigma is unknown.

questions.question: Write the test statistic for a one-proportion z-test.
solutionSteps: Center the sample proportion at the null value \(p_0\).
Use the null proportion in the standard error.
hints: Hypothesis-test standard errors use the null value.

questions.question: For matched-pairs data, what quantity is analyzed in a paired t-test?
questions.answer: d_i=x_i-y_i,\quad \text{then perform a one-sample t-test on the differences}
solutionSteps: Each pair produces one difference score.
The analysis focuses on the mean of those differences.
hints: Reduce each pair to one difference.

questions.question: What does the sample correlation coefficient \(r\) measure?
questions.answer: \text{The direction and strength of a linear relationship between two quantitative variables}
solutionSteps: The sign indicates direction.
The magnitude indicates the strength of the linear association.
hints: Correlation describes linear association, not causation.

questions.question: Write the form of a simple least-squares regression line.
solutionSteps: The intercept is \(b_0\).
The slope is \(b_1\), representing the predicted change in y for a one-unit increase in x.
hints: A simple regression line has one predictor.

questions.question: Write the formula for a residual in a regression model.
solutionSteps: A residual compares the observed response with the fitted response.
hints: Residual equals observed minus predicted.

questions.question: Interpret the coefficient of determination \(R^2\) in a simple regression model.
questions.answer: R^2\text{ is the proportion of variability in the response explained by the regression model}
solutionSteps: R-squared compares explained variation with total variation.
It is commonly expressed as a proportion or percentage.
hints: Focus on explained variability in the response.

questions.question: Write the chi-square test statistic used for a contingency table.
solutionSteps: Compare each observed cell count with its expected count.
Square the difference, divide by the expected count, and sum across all cells.
hints: Large discrepancies between observed and expected counts increase chi-square.

questions.question: Describe the basic idea of a nonparametric bootstrap for estimating the sampling distribution of a statistic.
questions.answer: \text{Repeatedly sample }n\text{ observations with replacement from the observed data and recompute the statistic}
solutionSteps: Treat the observed sample as an empirical approximation to the population.
Create many resamples of the same size using sampling with replacement.
Use the resulting statistics to approximate the statistic's sampling distribution.
hints: A bootstrap sample has the same size as the original sample.

questions.question: In a nonparametric bootstrap, should observations be sampled with or without replacement?
solutionSteps: Sampling with replacement allows an observation to appear multiple times in one resample.
This mimics repeated sampling from the empirical distribution.
hints: Duplicate observations are allowed in a bootstrap sample.

questions.question: Write the percentile-bootstrap confidence interval for a parameter using bootstrap estimates \(\hat\theta^{*(1)},\ldots,\hat\theta^{*(B)}\).
solutionSteps: Order the bootstrap estimates.
Use the lower and upper empirical quantiles corresponding to the desired confidence level.
hints: For a 95% interval, use the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles.

questions.question: Write the bootstrap estimate of the standard error of a statistic from bootstrap estimates \(\hat\theta^{*(1)},\ldots,\hat\theta^{*(B)}\).
solutionSteps: Treat the bootstrap estimates as an empirical sampling distribution.
Compute their sample standard deviation.
hints: The bootstrap standard error is the spread of the bootstrap statistics.

questions.question: Why is bootstrapping especially useful for a statistic whose analytic standard error is difficult to derive?
questions.answer: \text{It approximates the sampling distribution empirically without requiring a closed-form variance formula}
solutionSteps: Many statistics have complicated or unknown sampling distributions.
Repeated resampling provides a numerical approximation to that distribution.
hints: Bootstrapping replaces difficult algebra with resampling.

questions.question: Write the bootstrap estimate of the bias of an estimator \(\hat\theta\).
solutionSteps: Compare the center of the bootstrap distribution with the statistic from the original sample.
The difference estimates the estimator's bias.
hints: Bootstrap bias is bootstrap mean minus original estimate.

questions.question: Why can the ordinary nonparametric bootstrap perform poorly for very small samples or highly irregular estimators?
questions.answer: \text{The empirical distribution may poorly approximate the population, and the estimator's sampling behavior may not be well reproduced}
solutionSteps: The bootstrap relies on the observed sample representing the population adequately.
Nonsmooth statistics or boundary parameters may violate regularity conditions.
hints: Bootstrap accuracy depends on both sample size and estimator behavior.

questions.question: Describe the null hypothesis represented by a two-group permutation test that randomly reassigns group labels.
questions.answer: \text{Under }H_0,\text{ the outcomes are exchangeable across groups and the group labels carry no systematic effect}
solutionSteps: Permutation tests generate a null distribution by relabeling observations.
This is justified when labels are interchangeable under the null.
hints: Think of the null as no meaningful connection between labels and outcomes.

questions.question: Write the general permutation-test \(p\)-value using \(B\) permuted statistics and an observed statistic \(T_{\mathrm{obs}}\).
solutionSteps: Count the permuted statistics at least as extreme as the observed statistic.
The plus-one adjustment prevents a zero estimated \(p\)-value.
hints: For a two-sided test, compare absolute values.

questions.question: Describe the leave-one-out jackknife procedure for a statistic \(\hat\theta\).
questions.answer: \text{Recompute }\hat\theta\text{ }n\text{ times, each time omitting one observation}
solutionSteps: Create \(n\) reduced samples.
Each reduced sample leaves out a different observation.
Use the variation among the resulting estimates to assess bias or standard error.
hints: The jackknife removes one observation at a time rather than resampling with replacement.

questions.question: Write the simple linear regression model for response \(Y_i\) and predictor \(X_i\).
solutionSteps: \(\beta_0\) is the intercept.
\(\beta_1\) is the expected change in the response for a one-unit increase in the predictor.
\(\varepsilon_i\) represents unexplained variation.
hints: A simple regression model has one predictor and one error term.

questions.question: Interpret the slope \(\beta_1\) in a simple linear regression model.
questions.answer: \beta_1\text{ is the expected change in }Y\text{ associated with a one-unit increase in }X
solutionSteps: The slope describes the change in the conditional mean of the response.
Its units are response units per predictor unit.
hints: Interpret the slope in the context of the variables and their units.

questions.question: Interpret the intercept \(\beta_0\) in a simple linear regression model.
solutionSteps: The intercept is the expected response when the predictor equals zero.
Its practical meaning depends on whether \(X=0\) is meaningful and within the observed range.
hints: An intercept can be mathematically valid but contextually unhelpful.

questions.question: Write the least-squares objective minimized to estimate \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\).
solutionSteps: Compute each residual as observed minus predicted.
Square the residuals and sum them.
Choose the coefficients that minimize this sum.
hints: Least squares minimizes squared residuals.

questions.question: Write the least-squares slope estimator in simple linear regression.
solutionSteps: The numerator measures how \(X\) and \(Y\) vary together.
The denominator measures variation in \(X\).
hints: The slope is a covariance-like quantity divided by predictor variability.

questions.question: Write the least-squares intercept estimator after the slope \(b_1\) has been obtained.
solutionSteps: The fitted least-squares line passes through \((\bar x,\bar y)\).
Rearrange \(\bar y=b_0+b_1\bar x\).
hints: Use the fact that the regression line passes through the sample means.

questions.question: Write the test statistic for testing \(H_0:\beta_1=0\) in simple linear regression.
solutionSteps: Compare the estimated slope with the null value.
Scale the difference by the slope's standard error.
hints: Simple regression uses \(n-2\) residual degrees of freedom.

questions.question: Distinguish a confidence interval for the mean response at \(X=x_0\) from a prediction interval for a new observation at \(X=x_0\).
questions.answer: \text{A prediction interval is wider because it includes both uncertainty in the mean response and individual-level error variation}
solutionSteps: Both intervals center on the fitted value at \(x_0\).
The confidence interval targets the average response.
The prediction interval targets one future observation and includes additional residual variability.
hints: Predicting one person is less precise than estimating a group mean.

questions.question: What residual pattern would suggest that the linearity assumption is violated?
solutionSteps: Under linearity, residuals should fluctuate randomly around zero.
Curvature indicates that a straight line is missing structure.
hints: Random scatter supports linearity; shape indicates misspecification.

questions.question: What residual pattern would suggest heteroskedasticity?
questions.answer: \text{A changing vertical spread, such as a funnel shape}
solutionSteps: Homoskedasticity means constant residual variance.
A widening or narrowing spread indicates nonconstant variance.
hints: Look for changes in residual spread rather than changes in the mean pattern.

questions.question: Write a multiple linear regression model with predictors \(X_{1i},\ldots,X_{pi}\).
solutionSteps: The model contains one intercept and one coefficient for each predictor.
The error term captures unexplained variation.
hints: Multiple regression extends simple regression by including several predictors.

questions.question: Interpret \(\beta_j\) in a multiple regression model.
questions.answer: \beta_j\text{ is the expected change in }Y\text{ for a one-unit increase in }X_j\text{ while holding the other predictors constant}
solutionSteps: Multiple-regression coefficients are conditional effects.
The interpretation compares observations that are equal on the other modeled predictors.
hints: Include the phrase 'holding other predictors constant.'

questions.question: A categorical predictor has four levels. How many indicator variables are usually included when the model contains an intercept?
solutionSteps: Choose one category as the reference level.
Create one indicator for each remaining category.
hints: Using all four indicators with an intercept creates perfect collinearity.

questions.question: Interpret the coefficient of an indicator variable \(D\) for a treatment group when the reference group has \(D=0\).
questions.answer: \beta_1\text{ is the expected mean difference between the treatment group and the reference group}
solutionSteps: When \(D=0\), the expected response is \(\beta_0\).
When \(D=1\), the expected response is \(\beta_0+\beta_1\).
The difference is \(\beta_1\).
hints: Compare the fitted means for \(D=1\) and \(D=0\).

questions.question: Write a regression model containing an interaction between predictors \(X_1\) and \(X_2\).
solutionSteps: Include both main effects.
Add the product term \(X_1X_2\) to allow the effect of one predictor to depend on the other.
hints: An interaction is represented by a product term.

questions.question: In the model \(Y=\beta_0+\beta_1X_1+\beta_2X_2+\beta_3X_1X_2+\varepsilon\), write the marginal effect of \(X_1\) on the expected response.
solutionSteps: Differentiate the conditional mean with respect to \(X_1\).
The interaction makes the effect depend on \(X_2\).
hints: With an interaction, a main-effect coefficient is not the effect at every value of the other predictor.

questions.question: How can adding a confounder \(Z\) to a regression model change the estimated coefficient of \(X\)?
questions.answer: \text{Adjustment can change }\hat\beta_1\text{ because it compares observations with similar values of }Z
solutionSteps: A confounder is associated with both the predictor and outcome.
Failing to adjust can mix the effect of \(X\) with differences in \(Z\).
hints: Regression adjustment changes the comparison being made.

questions.question: What is multicollinearity in a multiple regression model?
questions.answer: \text{Strong linear relationships among predictors that make individual coefficient estimates unstable or imprecise}
solutionSteps: Highly related predictors contain overlapping information.
This can inflate coefficient standard errors without necessarily harming overall prediction.
hints: Multicollinearity is a predictor-to-predictor issue.

questions.question: Write the variance inflation factor for predictor \(X_j\).
questions.expression: R_j^2=\text{the }R^2\text{ from regressing }X_j\text{ on the other predictors}
solutionSteps: Regress the predictor of interest on the remaining predictors.
Use the resulting \(R_j^2\) to measure how much its coefficient variance is inflated.
hints: A VIF near 1 indicates little linear dependence on the other predictors.

questions.question: Why is adjusted \(R^2\) often preferred to ordinary \(R^2\) when comparing models with different numbers of predictors?
questions.answer: \text{Adjusted }R^2\text{ penalizes model complexity, whereas ordinary }R^2\text{ never decreases when predictors are added}
solutionSteps: Ordinary \(R^2\) rewards even weak predictors.
Adjusted \(R^2\) balances fit with the number of predictors.
hints: Model comparison should consider both fit and complexity.

questions.question: Write the partial \(F\)-test statistic for comparing a reduced model with a full nested model.
solutionSteps: Measure the improvement in residual sum of squares from adding predictors.
Scale that improvement by the number of added parameters.
Compare it with the full model's residual mean square.
hints: The models must be nested for this test.

questions.question: List the main assumptions commonly used for classical multiple linear regression inference.
questions.answer: \text{Linearity, independent errors, constant variance, approximately normal errors for small-sample inference, and no perfect multicollinearity}
solutionSteps: Linearity concerns the conditional mean.
Independence and constant variance concern the errors.
Normality supports exact small-sample tests and intervals.
hints: Separate assumptions about the mean structure from assumptions about the errors.

questions.question: When can omitting a variable \(Z\) bias the estimated coefficient of \(X\) in a regression of \(Y\) on \(X\)?
questions.answer: \text{Bias can occur when }Z\text{ affects }Y\text{ and is associated with }X
solutionSteps: An omitted variable must matter for the response.
It must also be related to an included predictor for its influence to be transferred to that predictor's coefficient.
hints: Both outcome relevance and predictor association are required.

questions.question: How is a standardized regression coefficient interpreted?
questions.expression: X_j\text{ and }Y\text{ standardized to mean }0\text{ and standard deviation }1
questions.answer: \text{It is the expected change in }Y\text{ standard deviations for a one-standard-deviation increase in }X_j\text{, holding other predictors constant}
solutionSteps: Standardization removes the original measurement units.
This can aid comparisons across predictors measured on different scales.
hints: Standardized coefficients are expressed in standard-deviation units.

questions.question: Write a quadratic regression model for a continuous predictor \(X\).
solutionSteps: Include the linear term and the squared term.
The quadratic term allows curvature in the conditional mean.
hints: A quadratic model is still linear in its coefficients.

questions.question: Write the logistic regression model for a binary response \(Y\) and predictor \(X\).
solutionSteps: Model the log-odds rather than the probability directly.
The linear predictor can take any real value while the implied probability remains between 0 and 1.
hints: The link function is the logit.

questions.question: Solve the logistic regression model for the predicted probability \(p(X)\).
solutionSteps: Begin with the log-odds equation.
Exponentiate to obtain the odds.
Convert odds to probability.
hints: The inverse-logit maps the real line to \((0,1)\).

questions.question: Interpret the coefficient \(\beta_1\) in a logistic regression model.
questions.answer: \text{A one-unit increase in }X\text{ changes the log-odds by }\beta_1\text{ and multiplies the odds by }e^{\beta_1}
solutionSteps: Logistic coefficients are naturally expressed on the log-odds scale.
Exponentiating converts the effect to an odds ratio.
hints: Use \(e^{\beta_1}\) for the multiplicative effect on odds.

questions.question: Write the odds ratio associated with a \(c\)-unit increase in a logistic-regression predictor \(X\).
solutionSteps: A one-unit increase multiplies the odds by \(e^{\beta_1}\).
A \(c\)-unit increase multiplies the log-odds change by \(c\).
hints: Scale the coefficient before exponentiating.

questions.question: Why does a constant logistic-regression coefficient not imply a constant change in probability?
questions.answer: \text{The probability scale is nonlinear, so the same log-odds change produces different probability changes at different baseline probabilities}
solutionSteps: The logistic curve is steepest near probability \(0.5\).
It flattens near probabilities 0 and 1.
hints: Distinguish effects on log-odds from effects on probability.

questions.question: Write a multiple logistic regression model with predictors \(X_1,\ldots,X_p\).
solutionSteps: Use a linear predictor containing all predictors.
Apply the logit link to the conditional probability.
hints: Interpret each coefficient while holding the other predictors constant.
