questions.question: Write the Wald test statistic for testing \(H_0:\beta_j=0\) in logistic regression.
solutionSteps: Compare the estimated coefficient with the null value zero.
Scale by its estimated standard error.
hints: Large absolute Wald statistics provide evidence against the null.

questions.question: What does a likelihood-ratio test compare in logistic regression?
questions.answer: \text{It tests whether the additional predictors in the full nested model improve fit, using }2(\ell_F-\ell_R)
solutionSteps: Fit a reduced and a full nested model.
Compare their maximized log-likelihoods.
The statistic is approximately chi-square under standard conditions.
hints: The likelihood-ratio test is based on improvement in likelihood.

questions.question: Define sensitivity and specificity for a binary classifier.
solutionSteps: Sensitivity measures the true-positive rate.
Specificity measures the true-negative rate.
hints: Condition on the true class.

questions.question: What does the area under the ROC curve measure?
questions.answer: \text{The model's ability to rank a randomly chosen positive case above a randomly chosen negative case}
solutionSteps: The ROC curve summarizes sensitivity and false-positive rate over thresholds.
AUC measures discrimination rather than calibration.
hints: AUC concerns ranking, not whether predicted probabilities are numerically accurate.

questions.question: State the null and alternative hypotheses for a one-way ANOVA comparing \(k\) population means.
questions.answer: H_0:\mu_1=\cdots=\mu_k,\quad H_A:\text{not all population means are equal}
solutionSteps: The null asserts equality across all groups.
The alternative does not require every pair of means to differ.
hints: ANOVA tests a global mean-equality hypothesis.

questions.question: Write the one-way ANOVA \(F\)-statistic.
solutionSteps: The numerator measures variation among group means.
The denominator measures variation within groups.
hints: Large values indicate between-group variation is large relative to within-group variation.

questions.question: Write the decomposition of total variability in a one-way ANOVA.
solutionSteps: Total variation around the grand mean can be separated into between-group and within-group components.
hints: ANOVA partitions sums of squares.

questions.question: Write the degrees of freedom for the between-group and within-group components in a one-way ANOVA with \(k\) groups and \(N\) observations.
questions.expression: k=\text{number of groups},\quad N=\text{total sample size}
solutionSteps: Estimating \(k\) group means leaves \(N-k\) residual degrees of freedom.
The group effect has \(k-1\) independent contrasts.
hints: The two degrees of freedom add to \(N-1\).

questions.question: List the usual assumptions for classical one-way ANOVA.
questions.answer: \text{Independent observations, approximately normal errors within groups, and equal population variances}
solutionSteps: Independence comes from the study design.
Normality and equal variance concern the within-group errors.
hints: ANOVA assumptions concern errors, not necessarily the raw response alone.

questions.question: When is classical one-way ANOVA relatively robust to moderate nonnormality?
questions.answer: \text{When group sample sizes are reasonably large and the design is not severely unbalanced or dominated by extreme outliers}
solutionSteps: The central limit theorem helps stabilize group-mean behavior.
Severe skewness, outliers, or unequal variances combined with unequal group sizes can still be problematic.
hints: Robustness improves with larger, balanced groups.

questions.question: Why are post-hoc multiple-comparison procedures used after a significant omnibus ANOVA?
questions.answer: \text{To identify which group means differ while controlling the familywise error rate}
solutionSteps: The omnibus test only indicates that not all means are equal.
Multiple-comparison procedures address pairwise or planned contrasts with adjusted error control.
hints: A significant ANOVA does not identify the differing groups by itself.

questions.question: What family of comparisons is Tukey's honestly significant difference procedure designed to control?
questions.answer: \text{All pairwise comparisons among the group means with familywise error-rate control}
solutionSteps: Tukey's method is tailored to pairwise mean differences.
It adjusts for the fact that many comparisons are made.
hints: Tukey HSD is not primarily a method for arbitrary contrasts.

questions.question: Write the ANOVA effect-size measure \(\eta^2\).
solutionSteps: The numerator represents variation explained by group membership.
The denominator represents total variation.
hints: \(\eta^2\) is a proportion of total variance explained.

questions.question: Name the three effects examined in a two-way ANOVA with factors \(A\) and \(B\).
questions.answer: \text{The main effect of }A,\text{ the main effect of }B,\text{ and the }A\times B\text{ interaction}
solutionSteps: Each factor has a marginal or main effect.
The interaction tests whether the effect of one factor depends on the level of the other.
hints: Two factors imply two main effects plus one interaction.

questions.question: Write a two-way ANOVA model with an interaction.
solutionSteps: \(\mu\) is the grand mean.
\(\alpha_i\) and \(\beta_j\) are factor main effects.
\((\alpha\beta)_{ij}\) is the interaction.
hints: Include both main effects before the interaction.

questions.question: How should a strong interaction affect interpretation of main effects in a factorial ANOVA?
questions.answer: \text{Main effects should be interpreted cautiously because the effect of one factor changes across levels of the other}
solutionSteps: A main effect averages over the other factor.
When effects differ greatly across levels, that average can conceal important patterns.
hints: Examine simple effects or cell means when an interaction is present.

questions.question: Write a basic ANCOVA model with one categorical treatment indicator \(D\) and one continuous covariate \(X\).
solutionSteps: ANCOVA combines categorical group comparisons with regression adjustment for a continuous covariate.
The treatment coefficient compares adjusted group means.
hints: ANCOVA can be written as a linear regression model.

questions.question: What assumption is tested by adding a treatment-by-covariate interaction in ANCOVA?
solutionSteps: Without the interaction, both groups share the same covariate slope.
A nonzero interaction indicates that the covariate-response relationship differs by group.
hints: Parallel slopes correspond to no treatment-by-covariate interaction.

questions.question: When is Welch's ANOVA generally preferred to the classical one-way ANOVA?
questions.answer: \text{When group variances are unequal, especially when group sample sizes are also unequal}
solutionSteps: Classical ANOVA pools within-group variance under homogeneity.
Welch's method adjusts for heteroskedasticity.
hints: Unequal variances plus unequal group sizes are a key warning sign.

questions.question: In a simple mediation model, identify the roles of \(X\), \(M\), and \(Y\).
questions.answer: X\text{ is the predictor, }M\text{ is the mediator, and }Y\text{ is the outcome}
solutionSteps: The mediator represents a proposed pathway through which \(X\) relates to \(Y\).
hints: The mediator lies conceptually between the predictor and outcome.

questions.question: Write the two regression equations commonly used in a simple linear mediation analysis.
solutionSteps: The first equation estimates path \(a\) from \(X\) to \(M\).
The second estimates path \(b\) from \(M\) to \(Y\) while controlling for \(X\), along with direct effect \(c'\).
hints: One equation predicts the mediator; the other predicts the outcome.

questions.question: Write the indirect effect in a simple linear mediation model.
questions.expression: a=\text{effect of }X\text{ on }M,\quad b=\text{effect of }M\text{ on }Y\text{ controlling for }X
solutionSteps: The mediated pathway combines the \(X\to M\) and \(M\to Y\) paths.
Under a linear model, the product of coefficients represents the indirect effect.
hints: Multiply the two mediation-path coefficients.

questions.question: Write the relationship among the total effect \(c\), direct effect \(c'\), and indirect effect \(ab\) in a simple linear mediation model.
solutionSteps: The total effect can be decomposed into direct and indirect components under the standard linear mediation setup.
hints: Total equals direct plus indirect.

questions.question: Why is a bootstrap confidence interval often used for the mediation indirect effect \(ab\)?
questions.answer: \text{The sampling distribution of a product of coefficients is often asymmetric and poorly approximated by a normal distribution}
solutionSteps: The indirect effect is a product rather than a single regression coefficient.
Bootstrap resampling can approximate its sampling distribution without imposing symmetry.
hints: The product \(ab\) is not generally normally distributed.

questions.question: Why does a statistically significant indirect effect not automatically prove causal mediation?
questions.answer: \text{Causal mediation requires additional assumptions about temporal order, confounding, measurement, and model specification}
solutionSteps: Association among the variables is not enough to establish a causal pathway.
Unmeasured confounders can affect the mediator-outcome or predictor-mediator relationships.
hints: Statistical mediation and causal mediation are not identical.

questions.question: What is moderation in regression analysis?
questions.answer: \text{Moderation occurs when the effect of }X\text{ on }Y\text{ depends on the value of }W
solutionSteps: A moderator changes the strength or direction of an association.
It is usually represented by an interaction term.
hints: Moderation asks 'when' or 'for whom' an effect differs.

questions.question: Write a regression model for moderation by a continuous variable \(W\).
solutionSteps: Include both main effects.
Add the interaction \(XW\) to allow the slope of \(X\) to vary with \(W\).
hints: The interaction coefficient tests moderation.

questions.question: In the moderation model \(Y=\beta_0+\beta_1X+\beta_2W+\beta_3XW+\varepsilon\), write the conditional effect of \(X\) at \(W=w\).
solutionSteps: Differentiate the conditional mean with respect to \(X\).
Substitute the moderator value \(w\).
hints: A simple slope is evaluated at a particular moderator value.

questions.question: What does moderated mediation mean conceptually?
questions.answer: \text{The size of the indirect effect of }X\text{ on }Y\text{ through }M\text{ varies across values of }W
solutionSteps: An indirect pathway contains at least two component paths.
Moderation of one or more component paths can make the indirect effect conditional.
hints: The mediated effect is not constant across all cases.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of random assignment in an experiment?
questions.answer: \text{To make treatment groups comparable on average and reduce confounding, supporting causal inference}
solutionSteps: Random assignment balances observed and unobserved characteristics probabilistically.
It separates treatment assignment from participant characteristics.
hints: Random assignment supports causation; random sampling supports generalization.

questions.question: What is blocking in an experimental design?
questions.answer: \text{Grouping similar experimental units by }B\text{ and randomizing treatments within each block}
solutionSteps: Blocking controls a known nuisance source of variation.
Within-block comparisons can improve precision.
hints: Blocks are formed before treatment assignment.

questions.question: What is a \(2\times3\) factorial design?
questions.answer: \text{A design containing all }2\cdot3=6\text{ combinations of the two factors}
solutionSteps: A factorial design crosses the levels of its factors.
This allows estimation of main effects and interactions.
hints: Multiply the numbers of factor levels to count treatment combinations.

questions.question: Why are observations from the same participant in a repeated-measures study not independent?
questions.answer: \text{Measurements from the same participant share person-specific characteristics and are typically correlated}
solutionSteps: Repeated observations share the same experimental unit.
Standard independent-observation methods ignore this within-person correlation.
hints: Measurements within a person tend to be more alike than measurements across people.

questions.question: Write the paired-samples \(t\)-test statistic in terms of difference scores \(d_i\).
solutionSteps: Convert each pair into one difference score.
Perform a one-sample \(t\)-test on those differences.
hints: The sample size is the number of pairs.

questions.question: Write a random-intercept model for repeated observations \(j\) nested within subject \(i\).
solutionSteps: \(\beta_0+\beta_1X_{ij}\) is the fixed-effects component.
\(u_i\) allows each subject to have a subject-specific intercept.
\(\varepsilon_{ij}\) is the observation-level error.
hints: The random intercept captures stable between-subject differences.

questions.question: What distinction separates fixed effects from random effects in a mixed model?
questions.answer: \text{Fixed effects describe population-average coefficients, while random effects describe group- or subject-specific deviations}
solutionSteps: Fixed effects are common across the population.
Random effects model structured heterogeneity across clusters.
hints: Think population-average pattern versus cluster-specific variation.

questions.question: Write a random-intercept and random-slope model for repeated observations \(j\) nested within subject \(i\).
solutionSteps: The random intercept allows subjects to start at different levels.
The random slope allows the effect of \(X\) to vary across subjects.
hints: Random slopes create subject-specific trajectories.

questions.question: Write the intraclass correlation coefficient for a random-intercept model with between-cluster variance \(\tau^2\) and residual variance \(\sigma^2\).
solutionSteps: The numerator is variation attributable to clusters.
The denominator is total variation.
hints: ICC is the proportion of variance between clusters.

questions.question: Why can ordinary regression standard errors be too small when clustered observations are treated as independent?
questions.answer: \text{Positive within-cluster correlation reduces the effective amount of independent information}
solutionSteps: Clustered observations partially duplicate information.
Ignoring that dependence overstates precision.
hints: More rows do not always mean the same number of independent units.

questions.question: What is an influential observation in regression?
questions.answer: \text{An observation whose removal would meaningfully change fitted coefficients, predictions, or conclusions}
solutionSteps: Influence combines unusual predictor values and residual behavior.
An outlier in the response is not automatically influential.
hints: Influence is defined by impact on the fitted model.

questions.question: What does leverage measure in linear regression?
questions.expression: h_{ii}\text{ from the hat matrix }H=X(X^\top X)^{-1}X^\top
questions.answer: \text{How unusual an observation's predictor values are relative to the rest of the data}
solutionSteps: Leverage depends only on the predictor matrix.
High-leverage observations lie far from the center of predictor space.
hints: Leverage is about unusual \(X\)-values, not unusual \(Y\)-values.

questions.question: What does Cook's distance summarize?
questions.answer: \text{The overall influence of observation }i\text{ on the fitted regression model}
solutionSteps: Cook's distance combines residual size and leverage.
Large values suggest that removing the observation would noticeably change the fit.
hints: Cook's distance is a combined influence diagnostic.

questions.question: What does a normal Q-Q plot of regression residuals assess?
questions.answer: \text{Whether the residual distribution is approximately normal}
solutionSteps: Points near a straight line support approximate normality.
Systematic tail departures indicate skewness or heavy tails.
hints: Normality matters mainly for small-sample inference, not for the fitted mean alone.

questions.question: Describe \(K\)-fold cross-validation.
questions.answer: \text{Split the data into }K\text{ parts, train on }K-1\text{ parts and validate on the remaining part, repeating so each part is used once for validation}
solutionSteps: Each observation is used for validation exactly once.
Average the validation performance across folds.
hints: Cross-validation estimates out-of-sample performance.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of cross-validation in model building?
questions.answer: \text{To estimate generalization performance and guide model or tuning-parameter selection}
solutionSteps: Training error is optimistically biased.
Validation on held-out data better reflects performance on new observations.
hints: Cross-validation is about prediction beyond the training sample.

questions.question: Describe the bias-variance tradeoff in predictive modeling.
questions.answer: \text{More flexible models often reduce bias but increase variance, while simpler models often increase bias but reduce variance}
solutionSteps: Underfitting is associated with excessive bias.
Overfitting is associated with excessive variance.
Good prediction balances the two.
hints: Prediction error can rise at both very low and very high complexity.

questions.question: Write the Akaike information criterion and state its model-selection goal.
solutionSteps: The likelihood term rewards fit.
The parameter penalty discourages unnecessary complexity.
hints: AIC compares candidate models fit to the same outcome data.

questions.question: Write the ridge-regression objective function.
solutionSteps: Begin with the residual sum of squares.
Add an \(L_2\) penalty on the slope coefficients.
The penalty shrinks coefficients toward zero.
hints: The intercept is commonly left unpenalized.

questions.question: How does the lasso differ from ridge regression?
questions.answer: \text{The lasso uses an }L_1\text{ penalty and can set coefficients exactly to zero, while ridge uses an }L_2\text{ penalty and usually only shrinks them}
solutionSteps: Both methods trade fit for coefficient shrinkage.
The geometry of the \(L_1\) penalty enables variable selection.
hints: Lasso can produce sparse models.

questions.question: Define data missing completely at random (MCAR).
questions.answer: R\text{ is independent of both observed and unobserved data values}
solutionSteps: Under MCAR, missingness is unrelated to the variables in the analysis.
Complete cases behave like a random subsample.
hints: MCAR is the strongest standard missing-data assumption.

questions.question: Define data missing at random (MAR) in conceptual terms.
questions.answer: \text{After conditioning on observed variables, missingness does not depend on the missing values themselves}
solutionSteps: Missingness may depend on recorded information.
It should not depend on the unobserved value after that recorded information is taken into account.
hints: MAR allows missingness to depend on observed variables.

questions.question: Describe the main steps of multiple imputation.
questions.answer: \text{Create several plausible completed data sets, analyze each one, and combine estimates and uncertainties using Rubin's rules}
solutionSteps: Imputation reflects uncertainty by producing multiple completed versions.
Separate analyses preserve ordinary complete-data methods.
Pooling incorporates both within- and between-imputation variability.
hints: Multiple imputation is more than filling in one value per missing entry.

questions.question: What problem are heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors designed to address?
questions.answer: \text{They provide standard-error estimates that remain valid under nonconstant error variance, given other assumptions}
solutionSteps: Ordinary least-squares coefficients can remain unbiased under heteroskedasticity.
Conventional standard errors may then be incorrect.
Robust standard errors adjust the estimated covariance matrix.
hints: They change inference, not the OLS coefficient estimates themselves.

questions.question: Define the average treatment effect using potential outcomes.
questions.expression: Y(1)=\text{outcome under treatment},\quad Y(0)=\text{outcome under control}
solutionSteps: Each unit has a potential outcome under each treatment condition.
The average treatment effect is the population mean of the unit-level differences.
hints: The fundamental challenge is that both potential outcomes are never observed for the same unit.

questions.question: Define the propensity score.
solutionSteps: The propensity score is the conditional probability of treatment.
It summarizes observed covariate information relevant to treatment assignment.
hints: It is a treatment model, not an outcome model.

questions.question: What is the goal of propensity-score matching or weighting?
questions.answer: \text{To create treated and comparison groups that are balanced on observed covariates}
solutionSteps: Adjustment aims to reduce observed confounding.
Balance should be checked after matching or weighting.
hints: Propensity methods do not automatically solve unmeasured confounding.

questions.question: Write the Bonferroni-adjusted significance threshold for \(m\) hypothesis tests with familywise level \(\alpha\).
solutionSteps: Bonferroni divides the desired familywise error rate across the tests.
Reject an individual null only when its \(p\)-value is at most \(\alpha/m\).
hints: The method is simple and conservative.

questions.question: What error criterion is controlled by the Benjamini-Hochberg procedure?
questions.answer: \text{The expected proportion of false rejections among all rejected hypotheses}
solutionSteps: False discovery rate differs from familywise error rate.
It is often less conservative when many tests are performed.
hints: FDR focuses on the quality of the rejected set.

questions.question: Why should a statistically significant result not be interpreted as automatically important in practice?
questions.answer: \text{Statistical significance depends on sample size and uncertainty, whereas practical importance depends on effect size, context, and consequences}
solutionSteps: A very small effect can be statistically significant in a large sample.
Report effect sizes and confidence intervals alongside \(p\)-values.
hints: Significance addresses evidence, not necessarily magnitude or value.
